---
title: "PH718_Part_3"
author:
  - "Jasmine Lee"
  - "Zhiyang Zhou"
  - Jasmine (lee724@uwm.edu)
# date: "2026-02-17"
# date: "2026-02-16" to current date
#   -> creation_date: "`r Sys.time()`" # not '' it's ``
# date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
date: "`r format(Sys.time(), '%Y/%m/%d %H:%M:%S')`" # To update the date everytime
output: pdf_document
bibliography: ph718.bib
csl: apa-6th-edition.csl # Specify the citation style (e.g., APA, MLA, Chicago). The default style is Chicago Style (author - date). You can find more styles at https://www.zotero.org/styles.
---
# Introduction
R Markdown is versatile and can be used for a variety of purposes, including: documents, presentations, dashboards, and websites. In this document, we will provide a brief introduction to R Markdown and demonstrate how to include R code and plots in your documents.

# Installation of R Markdown

# Basics of R Markdown

## Headings: using "#"
- `# for level 1 Heading`
- `## for level 2 Heading`
- `### for level 3 Heading`
- `#### for level 4 Heading`
- `##### for level 5 Heading`
- `###### for level 6 Heading`
- `####### for level 7 Heading` (not recommended)

## Emphasis: using "*" or "_"
- `*italic*` or `_italic_` for *italic text*
- `**bold**` or `__bold__` for **bold text**
- `~~strikethrough~~` for ~~strikethrough~~ text

## Lists
- Unordered lists: using `-`, `*`, or `+`
  - Example:
    + Item 1
    + Item 2
      - Subitem 2.1
      - Subitem 2.2
      * Subitem 2.3
      * Subitem 2.4
      * Subitem 2.5
- Ordered lists: using numbers followed by a period
  - Example:
    1. First item
    1. Second item
        1. Subitem 2.1 - Have to indent two times to make it a subitem
        1. Subitem 2.2
        
## Links and Images
- Links: using `[link text](URL)`
  - Example: [R Markdown website](http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com)
  
- Images: using `![alt text](image URL)`
  <!-- - Example: ![R Logo](https://www.r-project.org/logo/Rlogo.png) - not gonna work -->
  - Example: ![R Logo](/Users/jasminelee/Desktop/MSDS/2026_Spring/PH_718/Rlogo.png){width=200px height=200px}
    - We need to have the image file on our computer and provide the correct path to display the image in the document.

## Code Chunks/Blocks
- Inline code: using backticks `` `code` ``
  - Example 1: The mean of the `cars` dataset is `r mean(c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5))`
  - Example 2: `r mean(cars$speed)` - Mean of the speed variable in the cars dataset.

- Fenced code blocks: using triple backticks ``` for larger code sections
  - Example 1:
    ```
    print("Hello, World!")
    ```
  - Example 2:
    ```{r}
    print("Hello, World!")
    ```
    
- Blockquotes: using `>` for quoting text
  - Example: `> blockquote`
  
## Extended syntax
https://www.markdownguide.org/cheat-sheet/

# Basics of R Markdown

## R Code chunks

R Markdown extends Markdown by allowing u to embed R code within document.
A R code chunk starts w/ backticks and `{r}`.

The answer to Q1 is:
```{r}
data(cars)
summary(cars)
```

The code for Q2 is:
```{r}
result = 0
for (i in 1:10^4) {
  result = result + i^2
}
print(result)
```

## Plots
You can also include plots in your R Markdown document. For example:
```{r}
data(mtcars)
plot(
  mtcars$mpg,
  mtcars$hp,
  main="MPG vs Horsepower",
  xlab = "Miles Per Gallon (MPG)",
  ylab = "Horsepower (HP)",
  pch = 19, # point character
  col = "cyan" # point color
)
```

```{r, echo = FALSE}
# echo = FALSE to hide the code chunk
data(mtcars)
plot(
  mtcars$mpg,
  mtcars$hp,
  main="MPG vs Horsepower",
  xlab = "Miles Per Gallon (MPG)",
  ylab = "Horsepower (HP)",
  pch = 19, # point character
  col = "cyan" # point color
)
```

```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.width = 6, fig.height = 4}
# fig.align can be the other option
data(mtcars)
plot(
  mtcars$mpg,
  mtcars$hp,
  main="MPG vs Horsepower",
  xlab = "Miles Per Gallon (MPG)",
  ylab = "Horsepower (HP)",
  pch = 19, # point character
  col = "cyan" # point color
)
```

```{r, eval = FALSE}
# eval = FALSE to prevent the code chunk from being executed
# include = FALSE to hide both the code and the output ?
# include = TRUE to include both the code and the output ? (default)
data(mtcars)
plot(
  mtcars$mpg,
  mtcars$hp,
  main="MPG vs Horsepower",
  xlab = "Miles Per Gallon (MPG)",
  ylab = "Horsepower (HP)",
  pch = 19, # point character
  col = "cyan" # point color
)
```

## Tables
You can create tables using the `knitr::kable()` function. For example:
```{r}
library(knitr)
kable(head(mtcars), caption = "First 6 rows of the mtcars dataset")
```

Another way is to resort to https://www.tablesgenerator.com/markdown_tables

| line 1 | line 2 | line 3 |
|--------|--------|--------|
|        |   ss   | dsafsa |
|   sd   |  feafd | dsafa  |


## Incorporating references using a BibTex (`.bib`) file

### Citing references

### In-text citation
Use the `@` symbol followed by the citation key of corresponding entry.
Example: @Wickham2016 will be used extensively later.

### Parenthetical citation
Example: We are going to extensively use the package `ggplot2` [@Wickham2016].

### Multiple citations
Example: Several `R` packages [@R2024; @tidyr2024; @Wickham2016] are fairly useful.

## Fenerate the Bibliography
# References
install.packages("tidyverse")
citation("tidyverse")
Example: Several `R` packages [@R2024; @tidyr2024; @Wickham2016; @tidyverse] are fairly useful.
